food_subgroup: Steamed whole fish with aromatics and spiced oil

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for steamed sea bass with green Sichuan peppercorns. FOODON_03302051 (steamed fish food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Sea bass (Dicentrarchus labrax and Lateolabrax japonicus), cooked
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers cooked sea bass as a closest parent. No specific code exists for the green Sichuan peppercorn preparation variant.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to this specific prepared dish.

serving.description: 1 whole steamed sea bass dish (shared, restaurant portion)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of steamed sea bass with green Sichuan peppercorns is approximately 400 g, representing a whole small fish dressed with spiced oil and aromatics, typically shared among 2–3 diners.

nutrient_source.title: Fish, sea bass, cooked, steamed — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 15060 adapted) and USDA Foundation Foods sea bass raw (FDC ID 173706), adjusted for steaming retention factors
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are based on USDA FoodData Central data for cooked sea bass (steamed/poached), with minor upward adjustment to sodium to account for soy sauce dressing typical of this preparation. Green Sichuan peppercorn oil dressing adds a small fat increment. Values represent a composite estimate for the whole dish per 100 g edible portion. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of protein- and fat-dominant foods — analogous estimate based on plain steamed fish (GI ~0–5); Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for steamed sea bass with green Sichuan peppercorns. GI is estimated at 5 (effectively negligible) based on the analogous category of plain steamed fish, which is a high-protein, very low-carbohydrate food. The University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 International Tables (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) confirm that protein- and fat-dominant fish dishes have GI values in the range 0–5. Glycemic load per 400 g serving is 0.1, reflecting the negligible carbohydrate content (0.6 g per 100 g × 4 g carbohydrate per serving ÷ 100 × GI 5 = 0.1).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce dressing
hypertension.guidance: Each 400 g restaurant serving provides approximately 1280 mg sodium, representing over half the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced soy sauce or consume a smaller portion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and high lean protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 0.6 g carbohydrate per 100 g and a glycemic load near zero, this dish has negligible impact on postprandial blood glucose and is a suitable high-protein, low-GI option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean fish protein with omega-3 fatty acids and low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sea bass provides omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and lean protein with low saturated fat (0.8 g per 100 g), supporting cardiovascular health; however, the sodium load from soy sauce should be moderated in patients with heart failure or hypertensive cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Sea Bass with Green Sichuan Peppercorns (青花椒海鲈鱼) is a contemporary Chinese seafood dish featuring a whole or filleted sea bass that is gently steamed to preserve its delicate, moist texture, then finished with a dressing of hot oil infused with green Sichuan peppercorns (青花椒), fresh ginger, scallions, green chili, and a light soy sauce. The green Sichuan peppercorn (as distinct from the red variety) imparts a characteristic mild numbing sensation (麻) and a bright, citrus-floral aroma rather than intense heat, making the dish simultaneously refreshing and gently stimulating on the palate. The flavor profile is described as 微麻微辣咸鲜 — slightly numbing, mildly spicy, and savory-fresh. In Singapore, this dish is served at Chinese restaurants specializing in Sichuan-influenced cuisine and is typically priced as a premium shared dish. Nutritionally, steamed sea bass is an excellent source of lean protein (approximately 18 g per 100 g), omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids, selenium, vitamin D, and vitamin B12, with very low carbohydrate content (0.6 g per 100 g) and low saturated fat. The primary nutritional concern is sodium from the soy sauce dressing, which can reach 1200–1400 mg per restaurant serving. Clinically, this dish is highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible glycemic load, and it supports cardiovascular health through its omega-3 and lean protein content. Sodium moderation is advised for hypertensive patients.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for this specific dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked sea bass data with a sodium adjustment for soy sauce dressing. The adaptation is well-supported and methodologically sound, but the absence of an exact match in a primary regional source limits confidence to medium.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods data for steamed/cooked sea bass, with sodium adjusted upward to approximately 320 mg per 100 g to reflect soy sauce dressing typical of this preparation. Fat values include a small increment for the hot oil dressing. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible for steamed fish with plant-based oil dressing; no trans fats expected). Glycemic index set to 5 (effectively negligible) as this is a protein- and fat-dominant dish with minimal carbohydrate; gi_category low per University of Sydney GI Database analogous fish category. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, all B vitamins, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 173706 (sea bass, raw) and NDB 15060 (sea bass, cooked) with standard steaming retention factors applied. Sentinel value -1 is not used in this record; all micronutrient fields carry estimated values from the USDA source. The dish is served at See Ya Tonight 今晚有局 (Singapore) at SGD 49.40 as a premium shared seafood course. Halal status is assessed as likely halal given the ingredients (fish, vegetables, soy sauce, sesame oil) but formal halal certification of the restaurant has not been verified.
